perturbation.original_text: The Breaching Party shall have sixty (60) days (thirty (30) days in the event of non-payment) after written notice thereof was provided to the Breaching Party by the non-breaching Party to remedy such default. Unless the Breaching Party has cured any such breach or default prior to the expiration of such sixty (60) day period (thirty (30) day period for non-payment), such termination shall become effective upon receipt of the written notice of termination by the Breaching Party to be given within ten (10) days of the end of such sixty (60) day period (thirty (30) day period for non-payment).
perturbation.changed_text: The Breaching Party shall have ten (10) days after written notice thereof was provided to the Breaching Party by the non-breaching Party to remedy such default. Unless the Breaching Party has cured any such breach or default prior to the expiration of such ten (10) day period, such termination shall become effective upon receipt of the written notice of termination by the Breaching Party to be given within three (3) days of the end of such ten (10) day period.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the cure period for a breach of contract to 10 days contradicts standard legal practice and may be deemed unreasonable, especially considering the complexity of potential breaches. Additionally, the notification period after the cure period has passed has also been reduced to 3 days, from 10 days.
perturbation.law_explanation: In California, a tenant is typically given a 3-day notice to pay rent or quit before an eviction action can be filed. This modified clause gives only 10 days to remedy any default. Many breaches may require significantly longer than 10 days to resolve, making the clause potentially unenforceable. The 3-day notification period is also violated.

(Amended (as added by Stats. 2020, Ch. 37, Sec. 16) by Stats. 2024, Ch. 287, Sec. 6.)
perturbation.justification: The modified contract reduces the cure period for a breach to 10 days and the notification period to 3 days. The Law Explanation states this contradicts California law, where tenants are typically given a 3-day notice to pay rent or quit before eviction. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 mentions Code of Civil Procedure and sections related to it, it does not fully detail the specific violation. However, the Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation state the reduction in cure period and notification period may be deemed unreasonable and potentially unenforceable, which serves as evidence that the change contradicts the law.

perturbation.original_text: Within [***] ([***]) days after the Effective Date of this Agreement, Aimmune shall issue to Xencor shares of Aimmune Common Stock (the "Shares") in accordance with that certain Stock Issuance Agreement, dated the date hereof, by and among Xencor and Aimmune (the "Stock Issuance Agreement"), and pay to Xencor by wire transfer of immediately available funds, into an account designated in writing by Xencor, an amount equal to five million Dollars ($5,000,000) (together with the issuance of the Shares, the "Upfront Payment"). The Upfront Payment shall be nonrefundable and noncreditable against any other payments due hereunder.
perturbation.changed_text: Within ninety (90) days after the Effective Date of this Agreement, Aimmune shall issue to Xencor shares of Aimmune Common Stock (the "Shares") in accordance with that certain Stock Issuance Agreement, dated the date hereof, by and among Xencor and Aimmune (the "Stock Issuance Agreement"), and pay to Xencor by wire transfer of immediately available funds, into an account designated in writing by Xencor, an amount equal to five million Dollars ($5,000,000) (together with the issuance of the Shares, the "Upfront Payment"). The Upfront Payment shall be nonrefundable and noncreditable against any other payments due hereunder.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the payment term to 90 days after the effective date can create issues under securities laws if the shares are not registered or an exemption is not properly filed within the legally required timeframe.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 15 U.S. Code § 77e - Prohibitions relating to interstate commerce and the mails
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 5 of the Securities Act of 1933 (15 U.S.C. § 77e) requires registration of securities offered or sold using interstate commerce unless an exemption applies. Delaying the issuance of shares for 90 days could violate Section 5 if it isn't compliant with registration requirements or applicable exemptions. If the shares are not registered and the exemption filing requirements are not met within SEC timelines, then section 5 is contradicted.

Prohibitions relating to interstate commerce and the mails (a) Sale or delivery after sale of unregistered securities Unless a registration statement is in effect as to a security, it shall be unlawful for any person, directly or indirectly- (1) to make use of any means or instruments of transportation or communication in interstate commerce or
perturbation.justification: The modified text changes the payment term to 90 days, potentially violating Section 5 of the Securities Act of 1933 (15 U.S.C. § 77e) if the shares are not registered or an exemption is not properly filed. Scraped Law Snippet 1 directly references 15 USC 77e, which addresses "Prohibitions relating to interstate commerce and the mails" and states that "Unless a registration statement is in effect as to a security, it shall be unlawful for any person, directly or indirectly- (1) to make use of any means or instruments of transportation or communication in interstate commerce". Delaying the share issuance for 90 days risks non-compliance if registration or exemption requirements are not met within specified SEC timelines. This represents an 'Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction' as the time-sensitive obligation doesn't align with legal requirements, making the changed term unenforceable or illegal.

perturbation.original_text: Xencor will be permitted to terminate this Agreement upon written notice to Aimmune, effective [***] ([***]) days after receipt of written notice thereof by Aimmune, if Aimmune or any of the other Aimmune Agreement Entities, directly or indirectly, (i) [***], or (ii) [***].
perturbation.changed_text: Xencor will be permitted to terminate this Agreement upon oral notice to Aimmune, effective immediately, if Aimmune or any of the other Aimmune Agreement Entities, directly or indirectly, (i) [***], or (ii) [***].
perturbation.explanation: Changing the termination clause to allow for termination upon *oral* notice *immediately* contradicts fundamental contract law principles requiring written notice for significant actions like contract termination. This change creates ambiguity and potential disputes regarding whether proper notice was given.
perturbation.law_explanation: California's Statute of Frauds (Civil Code § 1624) requires certain contracts, especially those involving significant financial commitments, to be in writing. While this agreement itself is in writing, altering the termination clause to permit oral termination directly undermines the purpose of the Statute of Frauds by allowing a crucial aspect of the agreement to be altered and executed without written evidence. A complex agreement is more vulnerable to misinterpretation and disputes if key actions like termination aren't formally documented. Also, this violates basic contract principles, which require a reasonable notification period.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 1624 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> Code Search >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 3. OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 2. CONTRACTS [1549 - 1701] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 2. MANNER OF CREATING CONTRACTS [1619 - 1633] ( Title 2 enacted 1872. ) 1624. (a) The following contracts are invalid, unless they, or some note or memorandum thereof, are in writing and subscribed by the party to be charged or by the party’s agent: (1) An agreement that by its terms is not to be performed within a year from the making thereof. (2) A special promise to answer for the debt, default, or miscarriage of another, except in the cases provided for in Section 2794. (3) An agreement for the leasing for a longer period than one year, or for the sale of real property, or of an interest therein; such an agreement, if made by an agent of the party sought to be charged, is invalid, unless the authority of the agent is in writing, subscribed by the party sought to be charged. (4) An agreement authorizing or employing an agent, broker, or any other person to purchase or sell real estate, or to lease real estate for a longer period than one year, or to procure, introduce, or find a purchaser or seller of real estate or a lessee or lessor of real estate where the lease is for a lon
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts the Statute of Frauds by allowing a critical aspect of the agreement, termination, to be altered and executed without written evidence. The original text requires 'written notice', whereas the changed text allows for 'oral notice', effectively eliminating the requirement for written documentation. The Statute of Frauds, as indicated in Scraped Law Snippet 1 (California Code, CIV 1624), requires certain agreements to be in writing to be valid. While the contract itself is in writing, permitting oral modification of the termination clause undermines the statute's purpose, creating a legally vulnerable and unenforceable situation. The change from requiring a notice period to immediate termination further exacerbates the contradiction, as it removes the reasonable notification period typically expected in contractual agreements.
